display_name: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
role_summary: Raylawni G. Branch (b. 1941): one of the first two African American undergraduate students to enroll at the University of Southern Mississippi (September 6, 1965, alongside Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong), accompanied to classes by six bodyguards across the integration year; Secretary of the Forrest County, Mississippi NAACP at the time of her enrollment. Jeanette Smith: Mississippi NAACP and Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) civil rights worker; participant in the same Hattiesburg-and-Forrest-County NAACP organizing.
ai_reading: Branch and Smith's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 115; the broader cluster includes Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96, also Hattiesburg NAACP), Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121, also Forrest County NAACP), and Freddie Greene Biddle (entry 44). The cluster is the Forrest County / Hattiesburg NAACP local-organizing cohort. The novel observation this joint page surfaces is the campus-integration-as-NAACP-strategy signature: Branch's September 1965 enrollment at USM was not a personal-trajectory choice but an NAACP-coordinated strategic decision to integrate the last major holdout of the Mississippi university system; the embedding catches that institutional-NAACP-decision register through her testimony as continuous with the broader Forrest County voter-registration tradition the Dahmer Sr. murder anchored four months later in January 1966.
biographical_paragraph: While the LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), Branch's external biographical record is well-documented through the USM Special Collections that hold the Raylawni Branch Collection [src: 1, src: 2]. Raylawni G. Branch (then Raylawni Young) was born in 1941 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi. In 1965, at twenty-four, she was Secretary of the Forrest County NAACP when the chapter recruited her to integrate the last major holdout of the Mississippi university system, the University of Southern Mississippi [src: 2]. On September 6, 1965 she and eighteen-year-old Hattiesburg native Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong enrolled at USM as the institution's first two African American undergraduate students; from September 10, 1965 she began attending classes, accompanied at all times by six bodyguards across the integration year [src: 2]. She was active in the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO), the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) [src: 2]. The Raylawni Branch Collection at the USM McCain Library & Archives, processed in 2023, contains her personal papers and movement documents [src: 2]. Jeanette Smith worked alongside Branch on the same Hattiesburg NAACP / COFO field organizing; her external biographical record is documented only through her LoC oral history co-interview with Branch [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Jeanette Smith
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked to open the joint interview with where she was born, Jeanette Smith places her roots in the Jones County community whose Civil War history shaped her family:
interview_snippets.quote: I was born in Jones County, free state of Jones. I was born January 1, 1940.

interview_snippets.speaker: Jeanette Smith
interview_snippets.lead_in: The interviewer asks Smith to explain the Free State of Jones for listeners who will not know the reference. Smith traces her descent from the Confederate deserter Newton Knight and Rachel, the enslaved woman he took as a partner:
interview_snippets.quote: Captain Newton Knight was able to meet my great-great-grandmother Rachel, his grandfather, and bought her as a slave. He fell in love with her. And he figured that he would just establish his own state. He didn't believe in segregation. He married her, and he had a family by her.

interview_snippets.speaker: Jeanette Smith
interview_snippets.lead_in: Invited by the interviewer to add to the account of the Hattiesburg movement, Smith argues that the local NAACP infrastructure, in place since 1946, was what made Forrest County safe enough to draw outside organizers during the 1964 Freedom Summer:
interview_snippets.quote: You had a group that had always fought for their rights. And we've had a branch in NAACP branch here since 1946. So the people came because it was safe.

interview_snippets.speaker: Raylawni G. Branch
interview_snippets.lead_in: Branch describes a Washington NAACP poster that documented her grandfather, Lee Val Graves, attempting to register at the same Forrest County registrar's counter where she herself would later register to vote:
interview_snippets.quote: And he and another guy were standing there at Theron Lynd's counter. The same counter I had gone to answer my Constitution question. I was registered first.

interview_snippets.speaker: Raylawni G. Branch
interview_snippets.lead_in: Branch recounts her own voter registration before registrar Theron Lynd, escorted by three NAACP men, answering the constitutional-interpretation question Mississippi used to screen Black applicants:
interview_snippets.quote: And he would walk back and forth and he would say, this is your question. Can you answer this constitutional question? And I wrote that. I wrote that. You know, Mrs. James had prepared us well on how on the Constitution. And I wrote and I wrote, finally he walks up and takes it and says, and handed it to a clerk and said, she passed. He didn't even read it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Raylawni G. Branch
interview_snippets.lead_in: On integrating the University of Southern Mississippi in 1965, Branch recalls a locker-room moment when white classmates looked for the tail they had been told Black students had:
interview_snippets.quote: I was putting on my shorts. And there was a big mirror. When I came up, I could see these girls standing behind me in the mirror, then watching for my tail. Because some people actually thought we had tails. And I guess they had been reared up with that lie. And I just smile. You don't pick a fight over something so silly. They could see I had no tail.

movement_context: Branch's September 1965 arrival at the University of Southern Mississippi sat at the institutional endpoint of a three-year federal litigation arc that had begun with James Meredith's September 30, 1962 court-ordered admission to the University of Mississippi at Oxford [src: 3, 4]. Meredith v. Fair (the federal injunction the Fifth Circuit issued to admit Meredith over Governor Ross Barnett's resistance) established the precedent that the federal courts would compel desegregation of Mississippi's public universities under the Fourteenth Amendment, and that injunction, combined with the Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VI prohibition on race-discriminatory practices by federally-funded institutions, made USM's continued all-white undergraduate enrollment legally untenable by 1965 [src: 3, 4, 5]. The Forrest County NAACP chapter, then under the leadership of Vernon Dahmer Sr. with Branch herself serving as chapter Secretary, identified the USM integration as a coordinated strategic action rather than a series of individual student-trajectory decisions; the chapter recruited Branch (who had attended Tougaloo College and was already a known local NAACP figure) and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong (an eighteen-year-old Hattiesburg native enrolling out of high school) as the paired test case [src: 2, 6]. Their September 6, 1965 enrollment proceeded under the federal injunction's protective umbrella; their September 10 classroom attendance, accompanied by six bodyguards, was the operational implementation of that institutional integration [src: 2]. The university itself records the date in its institutional history as the desegregation of USM [src: 7]. The Forrest County context made the action especially load-bearing: the same county had been the site of the long-running Theron Lynd voter-registration litigation that produced the first federal contempt finding against a Mississippi registrar in March 1962, and four months after Branch and Armstrong began classes the same county would witness the January 10, 1966 White Knights firebombing that killed Forrest County NAACP president Vernon Dahmer Sr. [src: 6, 8]. The continuity between Branch's campus integration and Dahmer Sr.'s voter-registration work was not coincidental, both were Forrest County NAACP chapter projects, planned through the same chapter meetings, drawn from the same network of Hattiesburg organizers that also produced Peggy Jean Connor (catalog entry 96), Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), and Freddie Greene Biddle (entry 44) [src: 6, 8]. Five years after Branch and Armstrong opened the door, Eddie Holloway (catalog entry 33) entered USM in fall 1970 as part of the early post-integration cohort; he would in 1997 become USM's first African American Dean of Students, the institutional continuation of the door Branch had opened in September 1965 [src: 7].
legacy_and_reception: Branch's post-1966 career carried her into nursing, the field she would build her professional life around following her USM years; she became a registered nurse and worked in Hattiesburg-area healthcare across the subsequent decades [src: 2]. Her continuing institutional record at the University of Southern Mississippi is anchored by the Raylawni Branch Collection at the USM McCain Library & Archives Special Collections, a processed archival collection (finding aid completed 2023) that holds her personal papers, NAACP correspondence, and movement-era documents [src: 2]. USM's own institutional history names her and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong as the university's first African American undergraduate students, and the university has held public commemorations of the September 1965 desegregation across the post-Civil-Rights-Movement decades [src: 7]. The arc from Branch's September 1965 enrollment through Eddie Holloway's 1997 Dean of Students appointment and his 1985-2000 Hattiesburg City Council service traces the institutional-integration trajectory Branch had opened, the same arc reflected in the city's broader transition to majority-Black-population Hattiesburg in the 2020 federal census [src: 7, 9]. Jeanette Smith's continuing biographical record outside the LoC oral history is sparse; her co-presence with Branch in the September 2013 LoC interview preserves her testimony to the same Hattiesburg NAACP / COFO field organizing through which Branch operated, but published external documentation of her post-1960s career is limited [src: 1].

gallery.alt: College Hall, University of Southern Mississippi, Hattiesburg, November 2016
gallery.caption: College Hall at the University of Southern Mississippi in Hattiesburg, the campus that Raylawni G. Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong entered on September 6, 1965 as USM's first two African American undergraduate students under a federal-court desegregation order. Branch began attending classes on September 10, 1965 accompanied by six bodyguards, a security arrangement maintained across her integration year. Photograph captured November 23, 2016.

gallery.alt: Bennett Auditorium, Lucas Administration Building, and Forrest County Hall, University of Southern Mississippi, viewed from the Johnson Science Tower, November 2016
gallery.caption: View south across the University of Southern Mississippi campus from the Johnson Science Tower, showing Bennett Auditorium (left), the domed Lucas Administration Building (rear), and Forrest County Hall (right). The Forrest County Hall building name preserves the institutional connection to the Forrest County NAACP chapter that recruited Branch as a chapter Secretary and coordinated her September 1965 enrollment as one of the university's first two African American undergraduate students. Photograph captured November 23, 2016.

gallery.alt: U.S. District Courthouse, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, April 2011
gallery.caption: The United States District Courthouse in Hattiesburg, Mississippi (built 1910, listed on the National Register of Historic Places), the federal venue where the long-running Forrest County voter-registration litigation against registrar Theron Lynd produced the March 1962 contempt finding, the first such federal judgment against a Mississippi registrar. The same federal-court infrastructure carried the post-Meredith university-desegregation injunctions under which Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong enrolled at USM in September 1965. Photograph captured April 28, 2011.

sources.title: Charles W. Eagles, The Price of Defiance: James Meredith and the Integration of Ole Miss
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 263894873 (Eagles's institutional history of the 1962 Meredith v. Fair federal injunction and the Mississippi-university-desegregation precedent that anchored the 1965 USM integration)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 37464286 (Dittmer's state-level history of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, the COFO Hattiesburg project, and the Forrest County NAACP chapter substrate that recruited Branch for the USM integration)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (Payne's chapters on the Hattiesburg COFO project, the Forrest County NAACP youth-chapter substrate, and the local-organizing tradition through which Branch operated)

sources.title: Patricia Sullivan, Lift Every Voice: The NAACP and the Making of the Civil Rights Movement
sources.publisher: The New Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 41368421 (Sullivan's institutional history of the NAACP including the Mississippi state-conference work of the 1950s and 1960s and the federal litigation that produced the 1962 Theron Lynd contempt finding)

sources.title: University of Southern Mississippi History
sources.publisher: University of Southern Mississippi (institutional history including the September 1965 court-ordered admission of Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong as USM's first Black undergraduates)

sources.title: Justice Delivered: The Vernon Dahmer Case
sources.publisher: Federal Bureau of Investigation, May 12, 2008 (FBI institutional retrospective on the 1998 Samuel Bowers federal conviction for the January 1966 Dahmer murder, with chronology of the Forrest County NAACP work that overlapped Branch's USM integration year)

sources.title: City of Hattiesburg History
sources.publisher: City of Hattiesburg, Mississippi (municipal history including the civil-rights-era integration of city institutions and the post-1965 transition that the USM desegregation anchored)
